reflection.quote: The world breaks everyone, and afterward many are strong at the broken places.
reflection.reading:
Saturday in late June. The longest stretch of daylight in the year, and for a lot of us in early sobriety, the hardest kind of day to fill. Friday night you can white-knuckle. Sunday morning you can hide. But Saturday — Saturday is wide open, and that open space used to be where drinking lived.

What I didn't understand at 22 was that the space wasn't the problem. The space was a gift I didn't know how to receive yet. Hemingway's line gets quoted to death, but the part people skip is the word *afterward*. Not during. Not while you're still bleeding. Afterward. Strong at the broken places means the work happened — the slow, unglamorous work of sitting with yourself on a sunny afternoon and not running. Cleaning the kitchen. Going for the walk. Calling the friend. Showing up to the thing you said you'd show up to.

A Saturday in June, sober, is not a small thing. It's a whole day of agency you didn't have before. You get to decide what it becomes. Hike, lake, nap, breakfast that takes two hours, a conversation that goes somewhere real. None of it is impressive on paper. All of it is the life. Eleven years in, I can tell you: this is what you're building toward. Ordinary days you don't need to escape from.
reflection.meditation: Sit for a moment with the day in front of you. Not the whole weekend, not the summer — just today. What is one thing you can do today that the drinking version of you couldn't? Do that thing.

affirmations: I can sit in an open afternoon without needing to fill it or flee it.
Today I get to choose what this day becomes — that choice is the whole gift.
I am building a life I don't need a break from.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a Saturday I get to remember. Grateful for the slow morning, the sunlight, the quiet ability to make plans and keep them. There was a long stretch when none of this was available to me, and I don't take it lightly that it is now.
gratitude.closing: One more day, clear-eyed. That's enough.
